entry.title: Classy JavaScript 🎩
entry.content:
I find it odd that, for example, classes were introduced in the language, since we already have a prototype system in place. I think that gives a shortcut to developers that are not willing to learn a different concept.

I’m a big believer that languages should have less facelifts and more consistency. Not only that, I also enjoy learning languages that teach new concepts, making me think fundamentally different in at least one aspects. Some examples:

  • Ruby taught me metaprogramming (open classes, dynamic method creation);
  • Swift taught me Optional types, protocol extensions and my first steps into functional programming;
  • Rust taught me ownership, algebraic data types and pattern matching.

On “How JavaScript Works”1, Douglas Crockford says:

“JavaScript also has more convetional looking class syntax that was designed specifically for developers who do not know nor ever will know how JavaScript works. It allows transfer of skills from lesser languages with no new learning.”

This is a rather strong statement, but it does have some truth to it. If you use JavaScript as you would use any other language, you’re missing the opportunity to learn new concepts, like prototypes and closures (of course, that also depends on your programming language upbringing).

Classless

Classes are mainly used to hold state and have an attached behaviour, while also providing type extensibility via inheritance and composition. Let’s try to have this semantics without the class keyword. The first idea is to use the prototype model:

function Counter() {
  this.count = 0;
}

Counter.prototype.up = function() {
  this.count += 1;
  return this;
}

const c = new Counter();
const count = c.up().up().count;
console.log(count); // 2

In this example, we create Counter (with capital letter to signal that it needs the new keyword in order to be invoked) with a state, a property count set to zero and a method that it’s attached to its prototype. What I like about this idea is that one uses the constructor for data and the prototype for behaviour, something like what Rust has with struct and impl or Go with struct and receivers.

One can tinker with the count property though (since it’s basically public), and being able to modify data directly is not good encapsulation. We should always define a contract, an interface for our classes / structs / objects. Functions to the rescue!

function counter() {
  let count = 0;

  function up() {
    count += 1;
    return Object.freeze({ up, val });
  }

  function val() {
    return count;
  }

  return Object.freeze({ up, val });
}

const c = counter();
const count = c.up().up().val();
console.log(count); // 2

Think about this for a second. We have state (the variable count) and we have behaviour (the methods up and val) as well. Not only that, but by controlling what we expose via returning Object.freeze, we effectively have private access level in our “class”! The bad thing is that every time we create a new counter object, we’re recreating all of its functions, and that’s why prototypes are good, because when you attach behaviour to them, every new instance comes clean with its properties. When you call a method, like c.up(), the JavaScript runtime asks the Counter prototype if it has something called up attached to it.

Ok, that solves grouping state and behaviour, but how do we introduce composition? Here’s another example with functions:

// Think of this constructor as an interface / protocol / trait
function flying(type) {
  function fly() {
    return `I am a ${type} and I can fly!`;
  }

  return Object.freeze({ fly });
}

// The "class" that "implements" the interface
function bird(type) {
  return Object.freeze({
    fly: flying(type).fly
  });
}

const b = bird('Canary');
console.log(b.fly()); // I am a Canary and I can fly!

We can include another function in our “class” to act as a building block, creating composition. Hopefully that shows that using “non traditional” methods to create custom data types is feasible and also cool to learn.

Using class

Here’s the Counter example using the class keyword and the new # symbol for private members2:

class Counter {
  #count = 0;

  get val() {
    return this.#count;
  }

  up() {
    this.#count += 1;
    return this;
  }
}

const c = new Counter();
const count = c.up().up().val;
console.log(count); // 2

Links

  1. How JavaScript Works ↩

  2. What’s new in JavaScript (Google I/O ’19) ↩
entry.summary: I find it odd that, for example, classes were introduced in the language, since we already have a prototype system in place. I think that gives a shortcut to developers that are not willing to learn a different concept.

entry.title: JSfy (all the things?)
entry.content:
Disclaimer: I started JavaScripting in 2016, already in the context of ES6 and the boom of React.

Fatigue or Renaissance?

Once I was talking to a group of programmers and one of them was getting started with JavaScript. After fiddling with the language, he said that the language was horrible, that it was too easy to make mistakes. Then, he went on to say that the language was ok, but the community, the people were awful. On other occasion, during an interview, the interviewer asked me “What do you think about JavaScript fatigue?”.

These situations made me realise that, at this point, JavaScript is so ubiquitous that everyone has something to say about it. People form their opinion after using the tools for some time, having dealt with the good, the bad and the ugly. On other occasions, we just propagate the louder voices of the community, sometimes spreading misconceptions.

I also remember reading somewhere “We’re not living a JavaScript fatigue, we’re going through JavaScript renaissance!”. You can make the case for both sides. Indeed, too many things are happening too fast in the JS community, and it’s hard to keep up with everything (frontend frameworks comes to mind). On the bright side, how incredible is the fact that there is so much activity all the time? You can literally find JavaScript libraries for all of your needs. And that takes me back to what my friend said before, that the community was awful.

The community effectively “ported” JavaScript to the server (Node.js), the desktop (Electron) and mobile (React Native). Whether you think it’s good to use JavaScript in these scenarios or not, that’s another story, but we must be able to admit that it is amazing how much people embraced JavaScript and made it the most popular programming language ever1.

You also have compilers that target JavaScript, allowing you to write code in other languages that eventually becomes JavaScript. See ClojureScript and Reason.

Furthermore, I don’t think JavaScript is a bad language. In its current iteration, I find the language (and its ecosystem) very convenient and fun to use. What Douglas Crockford wrote here2 pretty much sums it up:

“JavaScript is an astonishing language, in the very worst sense. Its astonishing nature led to a very bad reputation”.

“But despite JavaScript’s astonishing shortcomings, deep down, in its core, it got something very right. When you peel away the cruft, there is an expressive and powerful programming language there”.

Story time: Humble beginnings

JavaScript’s origin story3 is quite interesting. In 1995, Brendan Eich, at the time working for Netscape Communications, was tasked to develop a scripting language for the browser. The new language was going to be based on Scheme4, but then something happened. Sun Microsystems was about to close a deal with Netscape, where they would include Java in the browser as well.

In that scenario, Java is the big, enterprise language and they wanted a scripting language to be the lightweight counterpart. Available options at that time included Perl, Python, TCL and Scheme, but none of them resembled Java in the slightest. Netscape wanted to make the case that their language was up for the task.

That dramatically changed the fate of JavaScript, a language that initially was supposed to be “Scheme for the browser” now inherits the syntax of Java, with curly braces instead of parenthesis. Semantically, though, JavaScript still maintained some functional roots. A prototype was created in 10 days in May 19955.

It is easy to see how the language could have flaws, knowing that its first prototype was put together in 10 days, under a lot of pressure. It seems to me that the language might have been marketed as a less serious language, where Java got all the professional praise. It is also worth pointing out that during the period, the web was in its infancy, and JavaScript was not being used by professional engineers, but rather by anyone trailblazing the internet.

Interlude: Stockholm syndrome? 🇸🇪

Could it be the case that the more you use a tool, the more affectionate you become, thus making you blind to its flaws? 🤔

(Finally) Learning JavaScript

I’m under the impression that very few people actually bothered studying JavaScript. Most engineers I’ve come across just started using it and took it from there. It is a blessing and a curse that you can just pick up the language and most things just work. On the other end of the spectrum, Rust requires a lot of grinding before real productivity kicks in.

And there’s nothing wrong with either cases, they’re just different.

Being dynamic and weakly typed, JavaScript is very permissive, allowing you to create programs quickly, run them and see things happening, even if there are small mistakes lurking. The curse aspect is that one may never really understand the language. Common sources of confusion include: prototype, this and bind.

It became my quest to fully learn and understand the language, so I’ve read the following books:

  • JavaScript: The Good Parts (Douglas Crockford);
  • Effective JavaScript (David Herman);
  • Eloquent JavaScript (Marijn Haverbeke);
  • How JavaScript Works (Douglas Crockford);
  • JavaScript Allongé (Reg Braithwaite).

Both JavaScript: The Good Parts and Effective JavaScript deal with older versions of JavaScript (pre-ES6) since they were released in 2008 and 2013 respectively. One might think that these books are outdated and have little relevance, however I happen to like the history of things in general, so reading these books gave me a glimpse of the language’s past, while also providing some insights.

Yes, ES6 improvements certainly made some chapters less important, like the ones talking about "use strict"; and scoping with var (today’s const and let to the rescue!), but one still finds good advices, such as (chapters from Effective JavaScript):

  • 8 Minimize use of the global object / 9 Always declare local variables;
  • 11 Get comfortable with closures;
  • 18 Understand the difference between function, method and constructor calls;
  • 30 Undestand the difference between prototype, getPrototypeOf, and __proto__;
  • 34 Store methods on Prototypes;
  • 37 Recognize the implicit binding of this.

Notice that my favorite chapters are about functions and prototypes. Curiously enough, properly understanding the prototype model made me wonder why classes were introduced in JavaScript. Going forward though, it seems that classes are here to stay and the prototype model will only be wielded by those dealing with older environments (may it be browsers or libraries).

On Eloquent JavaScript, one can already sense that Haverbeke points to prototype as something just to be aware of, where classes are the way to go:

JavaScript’s prototype system can be interpreted as a somewhat informal take on an object-oriented concept called classes.

Apart from my personal feeling towards prototypes and classes, Eloquent JavaScript is probably the best (modern) introductory JavaScript book out there, guiding you not only through the language itself but also giving beginners a nice overview of networks, browsers, DOM, HTTP and Node.js. It is a very friendly intro to Web programming in general.

How JavaScript Works and JavaScript Allongé on the other hand, are geared towards advanced users. Crockford’s new book is a very ergonomic read, with chapters being just the right size and blending in a much appreciated dose of humour. My favorites chapters are:

  • 12 How Functions Work;
  • 17 How Classfree Works;
  • 20 How Eventual Programming Works;

JavaScript Allongé is a book to stretch your skills, rethink concepts and (hopefully) see things differently. It left me scratching my head a few times, but the aha moments were well worth it. You will gain a deeper understading of not only JavaScript, but programming in general, exploring different paradigms and techniques. Highly recommended as a “next level” book.

Epilogue: Lisp in C’s clothing?

Douglas Crockford called JavaScript “Lisp in C’s Clothing”6. By no means I’m a Lisp expert, however, from the limited knowledge I have about Lisp, Scheme and Clojure, there’s little resemblance to be found.

The key characteristic of the Lisp family of languages is homoiconicity7. From Wikipedia:

“A language is homoiconic if a program written in it can be manipulated as data using the language, and thus the program’s internal representation can be inferred just by reading the program itself”.

In other words, the code you write is the same code the compiler / interpreter sees and manipulates. That’s different from most languages, like C for example, that transforms your high level code into an intermediate representation before becoming machine code for the specific architecture you’re targeting.

Having “code as data” allows the following (also from Wikipedia):

“Lisp programs are written in the form of lists; the result is that the program can access its own functions and procedures while running, and programmatically alter itself on the fly. Homoiconic languages typically include full support of syntactic macros, allowing the programmer to express transformations of programs in a concise way.”

Simply put, a syntactic macro gives you the power to create new syntax for your language, and JavaScript does not possess that power. Then why people say “JavaScript is Lisp in C’s clothing”? Lisp pioneered some very important ideas in computer science and the ones that interest us are:

  • Automatic memory management;
  • Dynamic typing;
  • Higher-order functions.

On How JavaScript Works, Crockford wrote the following about closures:

It is the most important discovery so far in the history of programming languages. It was discovered in the Scheme language. It reached the mainstream with JavaScript.

I imagine that higher-order functions in particular weren’t that popular back in the 90’s, where C, C++ and Java were major players. So when people first laid eyes on this feature, it took them back to Lisp and Scheme and therefore this relation was made.

Links:

  1. The World’s Most Misunderstood Programming Language Has Become the World’s Most Popular Programming Language ↩

  2. Ibid. ↩

  3. A Brief History of JavaScript ↩

  4. Scheme ↩

  5. JavaScript ↩

  6. JavaScript: The World’s Most Misunderstood Programming Language ↩

  7. Homoiconicity ↩
entry.summary: Disclaimer: I started JavaScripting in 2016, already in the context of ES6 and the boom of React.

entry.title: First post 🎉
entry.content:
Hello, there!

This is the first entry in this blog. I plan to write mostly about Software Engineering and tips about productivity. You can visit the About section to get to know more about me and/or follow me on GitHub to see what I am up to.

While this is not really a first post (rather an introduction), here are some links to previous blogposts I wrote for two companies that I worked for while in Brazil:

Adopting new tech (Cheesecake Labs)

From zero to hero (mobLee - Portuguese only)

Divide and conquer (mobLee - Portuguese only)
